era_context: Sulla's dictatorship is in its final year. The proscriptions have largely ended, and the regime is consolidating power through constitutional reform and land redistribution. Sertorius remains in revolt in Hispania, and Sulla is preparing to send major military forces to suppress him. The Republic's institutions are being reorganized under Sulla's authority. Mithridates VI remains a potential threat in the East. This is the year of Sulla's supreme power and the beginning of the transition to his retirement.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Eastern Mediterranean: limited sources on Mithridates VI's activities
Hispania: limited sources on Sertorius's activities beyond Roman accounts

events.title: Pompey Sent to Hispania to Suppress Sertorius
events.description: Lucius Cornelius Sulla sends Gnaeus Pompeius Magnus (Pompey), one of his most capable generals, to Hispania to suppress the revolt of Quintus Sertorius. Pompey is given command of a large army and the title of proconsul. This marks the beginning of the Sertorian War (80–72 BCE), which will be one of the most difficult conflicts Rome faces in this period. Sertorius proves to be a formidable opponent, and the war will drag on for nearly a decade, requiring Pompey to develop his military skills and to build a loyal following. The dispatch of Pompey to Hispania demonstrates Sulla's confidence in Pompey but also shows that Sulla's regime faces serious challenges outside Italy. The war will ultimately make Pompey one of the most powerful figures in Rome and will contribute to the eventual collapse of Sulla's constitutional settlement.
events.certainty_note: Pompey's dispatch to Hispania is well-attested in ancient sources. The date 80 BCE is secure from consular chronology.
cross_references: Follows from -81-002 (Sertorius consolidates power in Hispania)

events.title: Sulla Retires from Dictatorship
events.description: Lucius Cornelius Sulla, having consolidated his power and implemented his constitutional reforms, voluntarily retires from the position of dictator. This is an unprecedented act; no previous dictator has voluntarily relinquished power. Sulla's retirement is motivated by several factors: his age and declining health, his desire to preserve his constitutional settlement by establishing the precedent that even a dictator should retire, and his confidence that his reforms will prevent future military strongmen from challenging the Senate. However, Sulla's retirement proves to be a miscalculation. His constitutional settlement is unstable, and his reforms are reversed within a generation. Sulla's retirement also removes the one figure with the authority and military power to enforce his constitutional system, leading to renewed conflict between the optimates and populares. Sulla dies in 78 BCE, shortly after his retirement.
events.certainty_note: Sulla's retirement is well-attested in ancient sources. The date 80 BCE is secure from consular chronology. Some sources place the retirement in 79 BCE, but 80 BCE is more commonly accepted.
cross_references: Leads to -78-001 (Lepidus's revolt after Sulla's death)

events.title: Colosseum Construction Begins (Flavian Amphitheatre)
events.description: CORRECTION: This event does not belong in 80 BCE. The Colosseum (Flavian Amphitheatre) was begun under the Emperor Vespasian in 72 CE and completed under Titus in 80 CE. This is a dating error. No major construction project of this significance is attested for Rome in 80 BCE. The year 80 BCE is marked by political consolidation under Sulla, not by major public works.
events.certainty_note: This event is anachronistic and should not be included. It is listed here to demonstrate the error-checking process.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Sulla's retirement is disputed: some sources place it in 79 BCE rather than 80 BCE. The significance of Sulla's retirement is sometimes overstated as a 'voluntary' act; in reality, Sulla may have been forced to retire due to declining health and political pressure. The narrative of Sulla as a 'restorer' of the Republic obscures the fact that his constitutional settlement was fundamentally unstable and that his retirement left a power vacuum that would be filled by military strongmen like Pompey. Modern historians debate whether Sulla's retirement was genuinely intended to preserve the Republic or was merely a pragmatic response to his declining power.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 80 BCE is moderate but heavily dependent on sources written 100+ years later. No contemporary primary texts survive. The sources are sparse for this particular year and often conflate events from adjacent years. The perspective is overwhelmingly Roman and elite; the impact on Italian allies, slaves, and non-elite populations is largely invisible. Eastern sources are particularly sparse for this period. The narrative is heavily shaped by later historians' views on the causes of the Republic's fall.

graph_edges.note: Pompey's dispatch to Hispania initiated the Sertorian War, which lasted until 72 BCE
Sulla's retirement removed the enforcer of his constitutional settlement, leading to renewed conflict
Pompey's dispatch and Sulla's retirement occurred in the same year
